statement.markdown: Given a string s, determine if every parenthese in s can be paired with another matching parentheses. For example, '(()' is not valid, but '()' and '(())(()' are valid. The input string s contains only three types of characters: '(', ')', and ')'. Your function implementation should use a stack data structure and return true if s is valid, otherwise return false. If you encounter an empty string, consider it as a valid string.
statement.inputFormat: A string s containing parentheses, of length n, 1 ≤ n ≤ 10^5.
statement.outputFormat: A boolean value true if s is valid, otherwise false.
statement.notes: You can assume that the input string does not contain any other characters besides '(', ')', and ')'.

hints.text: When encountering '(', push it to the stack. When encountering ')', pop the top element from the stack. If there is no element in the stack or the top element is ')', the parentheses string is not valid.

editorial.title: Valid Parentheses Stack Implementation
editorial.intuition: The goal is to ensure that every opening parenthesis has a matching closing parenthesis, which we can achieve using a stack. We push opening parentheses onto the stack and pop the top element from the stack when encountering a closing parenthesis. If the stack is empty when encountering a closing parenthesis, or if the top element is not an opening parenthesis, the parentheses string is not valid.
editorial.bruteForce: Brute force solution would involve checking every pair of parentheses individually, but this approach would be inefficient with a large string.
editorial.optimalSolution: Optimal solution involves using a stack data structure and iterating over the string once. This approach allows for efficient checking of parentheses pairs and handles long strings efficiently.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: Proof of correctness involves demonstrating that the optimal solution accurately detects valid parentheses strings and handles edge cases correctly.
editorial.dryRun: Step-by-step example run involves iterating over the string, pushing and popping stack elements, and checking stack emptiness
commonMistakes: Failing to correctly push and pop stack elements
Incorrectly handling edge cases, such as an empty string

starterCode.cpp: // TODO: implement a function called isValid that determines if a given string of parentheses is valid.
starterCode.java: // TODO: implement a function called isValid that determines if a given string of parentheses is valid.
starterCode.python: class Solution: def isValid(self, s: str) -> bool: pass

buggyStarterCode.cpp: int isValid(string s) { stack<int> st; for (char c : s) { if (c == '(') st.push(c); else if (c == ')') { if (!(st.empty())) { st.pop(); } } } return st.empty(); }
buggyStarterCode.java: public boolean isValid(String s) { Stack<Character> st = new Stack<Character>(); for (char c : s.toCharArray()) { if (c == '(') st.push(c); else if (c == ')') { if (!(st.empty())) { st.pop(); } } } return st.empty(); }
buggyStarterCode.python: class Solution: def isValid(self, s: str) -> bool: St = []; for c in s: if c == '(': St.append(c); else if c == ')': if not St: return False; St.pop(); return True;
buggyStarterCode.javascript: function isValid(s) { let St = []; for (let c of s) { if (c == '(') St.push(c); else if (c == ')') { if (!St.length) return false; St.pop(); } } return St.length === 0;
